title: Incorrectly Flipping Bits of a Number to Zero Count

statement.markdown:
Given a non-negative 32-bit integer n, you want to flip bits of n to produce a number of zero bits in the binary representation. Implement a function that returns the minimum number of operations to flip bits to zero.

Note: Any non-zero bit in the binary representation is to be flipped to zero, not a one.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum number of operations to flip bits to zero
statement.notes: You can flip bits at most 32 times.

examples.explanation: The binary representation of 5 is 101, flip the first and third bits to 000.
The binary representation of 7 is 111, only the second bit is non-zero.

hints.text: First hint text: Count leading zeros in the binary representation of the input integer

starterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: int minFlips(int n) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.java: public class Solution {public int minFlips(int n) { // TODO: implement return; }}
starterCode.python: class Solution: def minFlips(self, n: int) -> int: pass # TODO: implement

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: int minFlips(int n) { int res = 0; while (n > 0) { res += n & 1; n /= 2; } return res; }};
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution {public int minFlips(int n) { int res = 0; while (n > 0) { res += n & 1; n /= 2; } return res; }}
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def minFlips(self, n: int) -> int: res = 0; while n > 0: res += n & 1; n = n // 2; return res
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function minFlips(n) { var res = 0; while (n > 0) { res += n & 1; n = Math.floor(n / 2); } return res; }

solutions.python: class Solution: def minFlips(self, n: int) -> int: res = 0; leading_zeros = 0; tmp = n; while tmp > 0: leading_zeros += 1; tmp = tmp & (tmp - 1); res = 32 - leading_zeros; return res;
solutions.cpp: class Solution {public: int minFlips(int n) { int res = 0; int leading_zeros = 0; int tmp = n; while (tmp > 0) { leading_zeros++; tmp = tmp & (tmp - 1); } res = 32 - leading_zeros; return res; }}
